title: Smoke on Polyester
timeEstimate: 20–60 minutes home protocol; fire smoke = pro restoration
stainChemistry: Smoke residue on polyester is carbon particulate (soot), tar, oily condensates, and — depending on source — nicotine, creosote, or combustion-byproduct acids. Polyester is OLEOPHILIC, meaning it grabs the tar and oily condensate component aggressively and holds onto residual odor longer than cotton. Two cases: (1) LIGHT SMOKE — cigarette, candle, incense, one-time kitchen mishap — surface yellowing and loose soot that respond to careful cleaning. (2) FIRE SMOKE — house fire, apartment fire, smoke from a neighbor's fire — heavy saturation of soot, tar, and acidic condensates that have penetrated the fiber. Fire smoke clothing is a fire-restoration job (IICRC S500 textile track), not a home wash. The biggest mistake on light smoke polyester: hot water or hot dryer. Heat sets oily smoke residue into polyester permanently AND yellows the fiber. Cool throughout, every step.
whyThisWorks: Dry removal first lifts loose soot without smearing. Goo Gone (citrus solvent) cuts the tar and oily condensate that polyester holds onto so tightly. Dawn (engineered for cooking oil) emulsifies residual oil. Cool wash (NEVER hot) prevents heat from setting the residue into the fiber or yellowing the polyester. 3% peroxide on white polyester only oxidizes residual nicotine yellow without chlorine bleach (which damages polyester and yellows it long-term). The honest cap: heavy long-term smoker polyester or fire smoke polyester is often beyond home recovery.
defaultAssumption: Smoker household clothing, candle / incense exposure, one-time kitchen smoke, polyester garment / blend / kitchen apron / restaurant wear. Fire smoke clothing = pro restoration. Long-term smoker fabric = often plateau-prone; manage expectations.

spottingProtocol.instruction: FIRE SMOKE (any structure fire) = pro restoration, not home wash. LIGHT SMOKE (cigarette, candle, incense, one-time kitchen): continue. Heavy long-term smoker fabric: expect plateau, manage expectations.

spottingProtocol.equipment: N95 mask, disposable gloves, care label
spottingProtocol.instruction: N95 + gloves. Read the care label — most polyester is machine-washable cool. Dry-clean-only blends = pro. Open windows.

spottingProtocol.agent: Shake and HEPA Vacuum Loose Soot Outdoors
spottingProtocol.technique: Lift loose particulate before any moisture
spottingProtocol.instruction: Take the garment outdoors. Shake gently downwind. HEPA vacuum surface soot. Never damp-cloth first — moisture turns loose soot into a smear that's harder to remove.

spottingProtocol.agent: Goo Gone Pretreat for Tar Spots
spottingProtocol.technique: Citrus solvent on tar / nicotine spots
spottingProtocol.equipment: Goo Gone on white cloth, ventilation
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply Goo Gone to a white cloth — never pour on the garment. Tamp visible tar / nicotine spots from the back side with the front facing a clean towel (transfer pad). Hidden test on dyed polyester. Open windows, no flame.
Apply generous Dawn directly to spots. Soft toothbrush 10 min cool. Polyester is oleophilic — extended dwell needed. Cool rinse.

spottingProtocol.equipment: OxiClean per label, cool water, washing machine
spottingProtocol.dwellTime: 30–60 min soak + full cool wash cycle
spottingProtocol.instruction: OxiClean per label in COOL water 30–60 min soak. Then cool machine wash with detergent. Repeat soak if smell persists. NEVER hot — hot sets oily residue into polyester permanently.

spottingProtocol.equipment: 3% H₂O₂ on cloth or in soak
spottingProtocol.instruction: WHITE polyester only — hidden test on dyed polyester first. 3% peroxide on residual nicotine-yellow areas, 5–10 min, cool rinse. Skip chlorine bleach — damages polyester and yellows over time.

AIR-DRY only. Hang outdoors if possible. Smoke odor in polyester is the slowest part — VOCs diffuse out gradually over days. NEVER hot dryer until smell is fully gone — heat sets residual oily smoke + yellows fiber.

steps: 1. Fork — light vs fire smoke. Fire = IICRC S500 textile restoration.
3. Outdoor shake plus HEPA vacuum loose soot.
4. Goo Gone pretreat for tar / nicotine spots, hidden test on dyed.
5. Generous Dawn pretreat, cool, 10 min, soft brush.
6. OxiClean cool soak plus cool machine wash.
7. 3% peroxide for nicotine yellow on white polyester.
8. Air-dry plus multi-day outdoor airing for odor.
professionalProtocol.temperature: cool throughout — heat sets oily smoke residue into polyester and yellows the fiber
warnings: FIRE SMOKE = pro restoration, not home wash
NEVER hot water or hot dryer — sets oily smoke and yellows polyester
NEVER chlorine bleach on polyester — damages and yellows fiber
Polyester is oleophilic — Dawn pretreat is critical
Hidden test on dyed polyester before peroxide
Flammable solvent — no flame near Goo Gone

homeSolutions.instruction: Fire smoke (house fire, apartment fire, neighbor fire) on clothing is a restoration job, not a home wash. Soot, tar, and acidic condensates have penetrated the fiber, and the VOCs are health hazards. Restoration cleaners have specialized solvent baths and ozone for textiles. Light smoke (cigarette, candle, incense, one-time kitchen) — continue. Heavy long-term smoker fabric: this protocol helps but may not fully clear odor; ozone may be the finish.

N95 mask and disposable gloves before handling. Open windows. Read the care label — most polyester is machine-washable cool. Dry-clean-only blends go to a pro. Two hard rules for polyester: NO hot water (sets oily smoke residue forever) and NO chlorine bleach (damages polyester and yellows it over time).

homeSolutions.agent: Take It Outside and Shake the Loose Soot Off
homeSolutions.instruction: Outdoors, shake the garment gently downwind. HEPA vacuum (with brush attachment) any visible soot. Never damp-cloth or wet the fabric before this — moisture turns loose soot into a gray smear.

homeSolutions.agent: Goo Gone on a Cloth for Tar Spots
homeSolutions.instruction: Visible tar or heavy nicotine yellow spots: Goo Gone (orange bottle, citrus solvent) on a clean white cloth. Tamp the spot from the BACK with the front of the fabric over a folded clean towel that catches the transfer. Never pour on the garment. Hidden-area test on dyed polyester first. Ventilate. Goo Gone is flammable — no flame nearby.

Polyester is oleophilic — it loves the oily component of smoke residue and holds it tight. Apply a generous amount of Dawn dish soap directly to the spotted areas. Work in with a soft toothbrush for 10 minutes. Cool water only. Polyester typically needs 2 Dawn passes for smoke-oil component.

homeSolutions.agent: OxiClean Cool Soak Plus Cool Machine Wash
homeSolutions.instruction: Mix OxiClean per label in COOL water. Soak the garment 30 to 60 minutes. Then run a cool machine wash with normal detergent. Repeat the soak if smell persists. NEVER use hot water — heat sets the oily smoke residue into polyester permanently and yellows the fiber.

homeSolutions.agent: 3% Peroxide for Nicotine Yellow on White Polyester
homeSolutions.instruction: WHITE polyester only: 3% drugstore peroxide on residual nicotine-yellow areas, 5 to 10 minutes, then cool rinse. Hidden-area test on any dyed polyester first — peroxide can lighten color. Skip chlorine bleach entirely on polyester (damages fiber, yellows over time).

homeSolutions.agent: AIR-DRY ONLY Plus Multi-Day Outdoor Airing for Odor
homeSolutions.instruction: Hang to AIR-DRY — never the hot dryer until the smell is fully gone. Hang outdoors if you can. Smoke odor in polyester is the slowest part because the fiber holds VOCs tightly. Multiple days of outdoor airing can finish what the wash started. Persistent odor = pro ozone treatment for textiles.

steps: 1. FIRE SMOKE = pro textile restoration, not home wash.
2. N95 + gloves + windows open. Care label check.
3. Outdoor shake plus HEPA vacuum loose soot.
4. Goo Gone pretreat for tar / nicotine spots, hidden test on dyed.
5. Generous Dawn pretreat, cool, 10 min, soft brush.
6. OxiClean cool soak 30–60 min plus cool machine wash.
7. 3% peroxide for nicotine yellow on white polyester only.
8. AIR-DRY + multi-day outdoor airing. Persistent odor = pro ozone.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if this is fire smoke (pro restoration). Stop if smell persists after full wash + multi-day airing (pro ozone). Stop if hot water or hot dryer was already used (often permanent yellowing on polyester). Stop after 3 home cycles without progress.
warnings: FIRE SMOKE is restoration, not home wash
NEVER hot water or hot dryer — sets oily smoke + yellows polyester
NEVER chlorine bleach on polyester — damages and yellows fiber
Polyester is oleophilic — Dawn pretreat is the unlock
Hidden test on dyed polyester before peroxide

neverDo: Never hot water on smoke + polyester — sets oily residue permanently
Never hot dryer until smell fully gone — heat sets and yellows
Never damp-wipe loose soot before HEPA vacuum
fiberSensitivities: Polyester is oleophilic — grabs oily smoke residue tenaciously
Polyester yellows under heat over time
Polyester typically needs 2 Dawn passes for oil-component smoke
Polyester holds residual odor longer than cotton — patience and ventilation
Dyed polyester: hidden peroxide test mandatory

materialWarnings: Light smoke vs fire smoke is the whole fork
No hot water / no hot dryer — heat sets oily smoke into polyester forever
Polyester loves smoke oil — Dawn pretreat is the unlock
Odor is the slowest part — patience plus ventilation

escalation.when: Fire smoke (any source). Heavy long-term smoker fabric — years of saturation through the fiber. Persistent odor after full wash + multi-day airing. Hot water or hot dryer already used (often permanent yellowing). 3 home cycles plateau. Dry-clean-only polyester blends.
escalation.whatToTell: "Smoke on polyester [white / dyed], source [cigarette / candle / incense / FIRE SMOKE], age [recent / long-term smoker], home-treated with [HEPA vacuum + Goo Gone pretreat + Dawn pretreat + OxiClean cool soak + cool wash + 3% peroxide on white]. No hot water / no hot dryer / no chlorine bleach. [Persistent odor / yellow shadow / fire restoration needed]."
escalation.specialistType: IICRC S500 fire and smoke textile restoration specialist for fire smoke; professional dry cleaner with solvent and ozone capability for persistent light-smoke odor

contextQuestions: Light smoke (cigarette / candle / incense / one-time kitchen) or fire smoke (structure fire)?
How long was the exposure — one-time event or long-term smoker home?
Has hot water or hot dryer been used yet?
Is the issue visible spots, residual yellow, odor, or all three?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: The fire-vs-light split determines everything. Fire smoke = pro restoration. Long-term smoker polyester is the hardest light-smoke subset because polyester is oleophilic — it traps the oily condensate component and holds residual odor longer than cotton. Hot dryer at any prior step typically yellows polyester permanently and sets oily residue. Patience plus multi-day airing is the close-out — persistent odor needs pro ozone.
